passage:
Is there any knowledge in the world so certain that no reasonable person could doubt it? This question, which at first sight might not seem difficult, is really among the most difficult that can be asked. In daily life we assume as certain many things which, on closer scrutiny, are found to be so full of apparent contradictions that only a great deal of thought enables us to know what it is that we may really believe.

Consider the plain table before me. To the eye it appears rectangular, brown, and shiny; to the touch it is smooth, cool, and hard; when I tap it, it gives out a wooden sound. Anyone else who sees, feels, and hears the table will agree with this description, so it might seem that no difficulty could arise. But the moment we try to be more precise, our troubles begin. Although I believe that the table is 'really' of one uniform brown, the parts that reflect the light look far brighter than the rest, and some parts look white because of reflected light. If I move, the parts that reflect the light change, so that the apparent distribution of colours upon the table shifts. It follows that if several people look at the table at the same moment, no two of them will see exactly the same distribution of colours, for no two can view it from precisely the same point, and any change in the point of view changes the way in which the light is reflected.

For most practical purposes these differences are unimportant, but to the painter they are all-important: the painter has to unlearn the habit of thinking that things seem to have the colour which common sense says they 'really' have, and to learn the habit of seeing things as they appear. Here we already meet with one of the distinctions that cause the most trouble in philosophy — the distinction between 'appearance' and 'reality,' between what things seem to be and what they are. The table, if it is 'real' at all, must have some real colour; yet the colour we see is not a property of the table in itself but something depending upon the table, the observer, and the way the light falls. When, in ordinary life, we speak of the colour of the table, we mean only the sort of colour which it will seem to have to a normal spectator, under ordinary conditions of light, from an ordinary point of view. But the other colours which appear under other conditions have just as good a right to be considered real; and so, to avoid favouritism, we are compelled to deny that the table has any one particular colour.

The same holds for the texture. With the naked eye the grain of the wood looks smooth and even; under a microscope it shows roughness, and hills and valleys, and all sorts of differences that are imperceptible to unaided sight. Which of these is the 'real' table? We are naturally tempted to say that what we see through the microscope is more real; yet a still more powerful instrument would reveal something different again, so that we can never rest content with what the eye alone reports. Thus confidence in the senses, which had seemed so secure, begins to desert us.

It becomes evident that the real table, if there is one, is not the same as what we immediately experience by sight or touch or hearing. The real table, if it exists, is not immediately known to us at all, but must be an inference from what is immediately known. Hence two very difficult questions at once arise: Is there a real table at all? And if so, what sort of object can it be?

passage_source.name: Bertrand Russell, 'The Problems of Philosophy' (1912), Ch. 1, adapted

questions.stem: The author's primary purpose in the passage is to:
options: show that even an apparently simple object raises a distinction between appearance and reality
demonstrate that the senses cannot be trusted to reveal any truth about the world
argue that painters perceive objects more accurately than ordinary observers do
establish that the real table is uniformly brown, rectangular, and smooth
questions.explanation: The passage opens with a general question about certainty but immediately grounds it in the concrete table in order to develop 'the distinction between appearance and reality' — that development is its purpose (A). (B) overstates the text: the author says confidence 'begins to desert us' and still holds that a real table may be reached by inference, not that the senses reveal no truth. (C) misuses the painter, who is said to see things 'as they appear,' not more truly than they really are. (D) contradicts the passage, which denies the table has any one real colour or texture.

subquestions.stem: Does the passage stay abstract, or does it work from a specific object?
options: It works from one concrete object (a table) toward a broader philosophical point
It only ever discusses knowledge in the abstract
subquestions.explanation: After the opening question, the whole argument is built on the concrete table example.

subquestions.stem: What key distinction does the table example introduce?
options: Between appearance and reality
Between science and art
Between hearing and touch
subquestions.explanation: The author explicitly names 'the distinction between appearance and reality.'

questions.stem: According to the passage, we are 'compelled to deny that the table has any one particular colour' because:
options: colour is an illusion that exists only inside the observer's mind
only the report of a normal spectator under ordinary conditions should count
the colours seen under different conditions each have an equal claim to reality, so none can be privileged
observers disagree about the table's shape rather than about its colour
questions.explanation: The author states that the colours appearing under other conditions 'have just as good a right to be considered real,' so that 'to avoid favouritism' no single colour can be assigned (C). (A) is never claimed; the author says colour depends on table, observer, and light, not that it is mere illusion. (B) inverts the point — the normal-spectator report is a convenient convention, not the reason the table lacks one real colour. (D) misattributes the disagreement to shape.

subquestions.stem: Do colours seen under different conditions have different claims to being 'real'?
options: No — each has 'just as good a right to be considered real'
Yes — the brightest appearance is the most real
subquestions.explanation: The passage grants every appearance an equal right to be called real.

subquestions.stem: If no single condition's colour is privileged, what follows about 'the' colour of the table?
options: It cannot be assigned any one particular colour
Its true colour is the average of all its appearances
subquestions.explanation: Refusing to favour any appearance forces the denial of a single real colour.

questions.stem: Within the argument, the reference to the microscope serves chiefly to:
options: prove that scientific instruments finally reveal the real table
show that the table's roughness is more real than its smoothness
suggest that only trained scientists can perceive reality
extend the appearance/reality problem from colour to texture, undermining reliance on the naked eye
questions.explanation: The microscope discussion moves the problem from colour to texture, showing the naked eye is no more authoritative than the instrument (D). (B) is the tempting trap the author explicitly rejects: 'a still more powerful instrument would reveal something different again,' so roughness is not 'more real.' (A) is rejected for the same reason. (C) about scientists is unsupported.

subquestions.stem: Before the microscope, which property was under discussion? After it?
options: Colour first; then texture
Only shape throughout
Only the sound of the table
subquestions.explanation: The microscope paragraph shifts the same worry onto texture.

subquestions.stem: Does the author treat the microscope's image as the 'real' table?
options: No — a still more powerful instrument would show something different again
Yes — the microscope reveals the table as it really is
subquestions.explanation: Each stronger instrument yields yet another appearance, so none is final.

questions.stem: The author's attitude toward the reliability of the senses is best described as:
options: unqualified trust in their deliverances
initial confidence giving way to reasoned doubt
contemptuous dismissal of everything they report
complete indifference to the question
questions.explanation: Confidence in the senses 'had seemed so secure' but 'begins to desert us' — a movement from confidence to doubt (B). (A) and (C) are opposite extremes the measured prose avoids; the author neither fully trusts nor sneeringly dismisses the senses. (D) ignores the author's evident, careful engagement with the question.

subquestions.stem: At the outset, how secure did confidence in the senses seem?
options: Very secure
Already destroyed
subquestions.explanation: The passage says that confidence 'had seemed so secure.'

subquestions.stem: By the end of the passage, what happens to that confidence?
options: It 'begins to desert us'
It is fully confirmed
subquestions.explanation: The closing movement is toward doubt, not confirmation.

questions.stem: Two observers standing in different places both insist they see 'the same brown table.' The author would most likely respond that they:
options: agree only about the colour a normal spectator would report under ordinary conditions, not about identical immediate experiences
are both simply mistaken about whether the table exists at all
cannot both be right: one perceives the real table and the other an illusion
have proven, by their agreement, that the table has one real colour after all
questions.explanation: The passage says that in ordinary life 'the colour of the table' means only the colour a normal spectator would report under ordinary conditions; since 'no two can view it from precisely the same point,' they cannot share identical immediate experiences (A). (B) overstates — existence is a separate question raised only at the very end. (C) imposes a real/illusion split the author avoids. (D) contradicts the denial that the table has one real colour.

subquestions.stem: When ordinary people speak of 'the colour of the table,' what does the passage say they mean?
options: The colour a normal spectator would report under ordinary conditions
Their private immediate sensation, which is identical for everyone
subquestions.explanation: The author defines the everyday phrase in terms of a normal spectator's report.

subquestions.stem: Can two people in different positions have identical immediate colour-experiences here?
options: No — no two can view it from precisely the same point
Yes — position makes no difference to what is seen
subquestions.explanation: Different viewpoints necessarily change the reflected light and thus the appearance.

questions.stem: Which finding, if true, would most WEAKEN the author's claim that the real table 'must be an inference from what is immediately known'?
options: A more powerful microscope reveals yet another surface texture
People from different cultures use different words for the table's colour
Observers can, under controlled conditions, directly perceive the table's intrinsic properties without any inference
The table looks brighter in the places where the light reflects off it
questions.explanation: The claim is that the real table is reached only by inference from immediate experience; a demonstration that its intrinsic properties can be perceived directly would remove the need for inference and thus weaken it (C). (A) and (D) are simply further appearances that support the author. (B) concerns vocabulary, not whether perception is inferential.

subquestions.stem: State the claim to be weakened.
options: The real table is known only by inference, never immediately
The table is a shade of brown
subquestions.explanation: Identify the target claim before testing what would undercut it.

subquestions.stem: Which kind of finding would undercut 'known only by inference'?
options: Evidence that the table's real properties can be perceived directly
Evidence of yet another appearance under a new instrument
subquestions.explanation: Direct perception would bypass the inference the claim insists upon; another appearance would only reinforce it.

passage:
Among the most contested claims in moral philosophy is the assertion that ethical judgments are irreducibly relative to the cultural frameworks that produce them. Cultural relativism, in its strongest form, holds not merely that different societies happen to endorse different moral codes, but that no standpoint outside all cultures exists from which those codes can be objectively ranked. If morality is constituted by social practice rather than discovered through reason or intuition, then condemning another culture's practices amounts to little more than projecting one's own conventions onto alien terrain.

Defenders of this position often draw strength from the anthropological record, which reveals a startling diversity of moral norms across societies separated by time and geography. Practices that one tradition regards as obligatory — ancestor veneration, dowry exchange, arranged marriage — strike others as superstitious, exploitative, or coercive. From this diversity, relativists infer that no norm enjoys a privileged status independent of its cultural home. To insist otherwise, they argue, is to mistake familiarity for objectivity.

Yet the relativist position encounters formidable internal difficulties. If moral judgments are valid only within the culture that generates them, then the reformer who criticizes her own society's practices — the abolitionist in a slave-holding nation, the suffragette in a society that bars women from public life — is simply wrong by definition. Her protest cannot be morally correct because the dominant convention of her society says otherwise. This consequence strikes many philosophers as a reductio ad absurdum: a moral theory that silences the reformer and vindicates the oppressor cannot be adequate.

Universalists reply that moral diversity, while real, does not demonstrate moral relativism any more than disagreement among scientists demonstrates that there are no scientific facts. People may disagree about the boiling point of water and still be wrong; analogously, cultures may disagree about the permissibility of torture yet one of them may be mistaken. The universalist identifies certain core norms — prohibitions on gratuitous cruelty, requirements of basic reciprocity — that appear in some form across virtually every known society, suggesting not conventional agreement but convergent recognition of genuine moral truths.

A nuanced middle position distinguishes between first-order moral claims (what is right or wrong) and metaethical claims (what makes moral judgments true or false). One may acknowledge deep cultural variation in first-order norms while still holding that some metaethical framework — natural law, contractualism, or sentimentalism — provides a transcultural criterion of moral evaluation. On this view, the diversity of moral codes is evidence of human fallibility and limited information, not of the absence of any moral standard. Whether such a criterion is ultimately defensible remains among the central unresolved questions of moral philosophy.

questions.stem: According to the passage, the strongest form of cultural relativism claims that:
options: no perspective exists from which different moral codes can be objectively compared
all moral codes are equally good and should be respected without criticism
cultural diversity proves that moral reformers are always mistaken
anthropological fieldwork has confirmed that moral universalism is false
questions.explanation: (A) is correct: the passage defines strong relativism as the view that 'no standpoint outside all cultures exists from which those codes can be objectively ranked.' (B) conflates relativism with a tolerationist prescription the passage does not assert. (C) is a consequence the passage attributes to relativism as a criticism (the reformer problem), not a definition of relativism. (D) misrepresents the argument: the passage says relativists 'draw strength' from anthropological diversity but does not claim fieldwork conclusively confirms relativism.

subquestions.stem: What does the passage say the strongest relativist claim is about?
options: The absence of any neutral vantage point to rank moral codes
The equal practical value of every moral code
subquestions.explanation: The passage's definition focuses on the impossibility of an objective ranking standpoint.

subquestions.stem: Does the passage present anthropological diversity as proving relativism or merely supporting it?
options: As evidence relativists cite in their favor, not as proof
As conclusive proof that relativism is true
subquestions.explanation: The passage says relativists 'draw strength from' the record, not that it proves their view.

questions.stem: The author introduces the abolitionist and suffragette examples primarily to:
options: illustrate a consequence of relativism that the author implies undermines the theory
show that moral reformers are typically proven correct by history
demonstrate that strong moral convictions can survive cultural disapproval
argue that relativism is incompatible with historical progress
questions.explanation: (A) is correct: the examples are deployed to generate the 'reductio ad absurdum' — if relativism is true, reformers who challenge their own society's norms are by definition wrong, which the author calls a devastating consequence. (B) and (D) introduce historical-progress claims the passage does not make. (C) is plausible but too psychological; the passage uses the examples as a logical critique of relativism, not a comment on reformers' personal resolve.

subquestions.stem: What happens to internal reformers if strong relativism is true?
options: They are, by definition, morally wrong because their society's dominant norm says so
They are justified because they appeal to universal human rights
subquestions.explanation: The passage explicitly states that on relativism the reformer 'cannot be morally correct because the dominant convention of her society says otherwise.'

subquestions.stem: What label does the author give to this consequence of relativism?
options: A reductio ad absurdum
A pragmatic paradox
An empirical anomaly
subquestions.explanation: The author calls it 'a reductio ad absurdum: a moral theory that silences the reformer.'

questions.stem: The universalist analogy comparing moral disagreement to scientific disagreement is intended to show that:
options: disagreement about a claim does not prove there is no fact of the matter
moral philosophy should adopt the methods of natural science
cultures that disagree about morality are engaged in pseudo-disputes
moral progress follows the same pattern as scientific progress
questions.explanation: (A) is correct: the universalist uses the boiling-point example to argue that 'disagreement among scientists [does not demonstrate] that there are no scientific facts,' and the same applies to moral facts. (B) is not implied — the analogy concerns the logic of disagreement, not methodology. (C) inverts the point. (D) introduces a developmental claim the passage does not assert.

subquestions.stem: In the universalist's analogy, what corresponds to moral disagreement?
options: Scientific disagreement about an empirical fact like the boiling point of water
Political disagreement about which party should govern
subquestions.explanation: The passage draws the explicit parallel: scientists may disagree about the boiling point yet one can be wrong.

subquestions.stem: What conclusion does the universalist draw from this analogy?
options: Moral disagreement is compatible with there being genuine moral facts
Cultures that disagree about morality are equally correct
subquestions.explanation: The logic is: disagreement does not equal no fact; therefore moral disagreement does not prove moral relativism.

questions.stem: The 'nuanced middle position' described in the final paragraph holds that:
options: variation in first-order norms may coexist with a transcultural metaethical criterion
both relativism and universalism are ultimately incoherent and should be abandoned
metaethical frameworks must be derived from anthropological observation
cultural diversity in moral norms provides strong evidence against all metaethical theories
questions.explanation: (A) is correct: the middle position separates first-order questions (what is right) from metaethical questions (what makes judgments true), allowing cultural variation at the first level while preserving a universal standard at the second. (B) is not stated. (C) reverses the relationship. (D) contradicts the passage, which interprets diversity as evidence of fallibility.

subquestions.stem: What two levels of moral inquiry does the middle position distinguish?
options: First-order claims (what is right) and metaethical claims (what makes judgments true)
Descriptive claims (what cultures do) and normative claims (what cultures should do)
subquestions.explanation: The passage explicitly names 'first-order moral claims' and 'metaethical claims' as the two levels.

subquestions.stem: How does this middle position interpret widespread moral disagreement?
options: As evidence of human fallibility and limited information, not the absence of moral standards
As proof that moral relativism is correct at both levels
subquestions.explanation: The passage states: 'diversity of moral codes is evidence of human fallibility and limited information, not of the absence of any moral standard.'

questions.stem: A moral relativist would be MOST likely to find which of the following arguments persuasive?
options: The fact that widespread cross-cultural prohibitions on murder exist suggests those prohibitions track a genuine moral truth
A society that endorses human sacrifice is simply expressing different values, not making an error
Reformers who oppose slavery in slave-holding societies demonstrate that universal moral standards can be recognized
Moral disagreement across cultures arises from factual disagreements rather than genuine differences in values
questions.explanation: (B) is correct: relativists hold that a culture's norms are valid within that culture, so a society endorsing human sacrifice is just expressing different values — not wrong. (A) supports universalism by treating cross-cultural convergence as evidence of moral truth. (C) supports universalism by validating the reformer who appeals to universal standards. (D) is a universalist move: if disagreements are merely factual, underlying moral agreement could be reached.

subquestions.stem: What is the core relativist claim about a culture's norms?
options: They are valid within their cultural context and cannot be objectively ranked against others
They are valid only if they agree with universal human rights standards
subquestions.explanation: Relativism holds norms are valid relative to their cultural home, with no external ranking possible.

subquestions.stem: Why does option (A) support universalism rather than relativism?
options: Because it treats cross-cultural convergence as evidence of an objective moral truth
Because it treats cultural diversity as evidence of moral relativism
subquestions.explanation: Finding cross-cultural prohibitions and inferring they reflect a moral truth is exactly the universalist move.

questions.stem: Which finding, if true, would most weaken the relativist's use of anthropological diversity as evidence for relativism?
options: Anthropologists routinely misinterpret foreign practices because of their own cultural biases
Despite surface variation, all known societies share a core prohibition on inflicting suffering for no reason
The academic discipline of anthropology originated in colonial contexts that shaped its methods
Some moral philosophers argue that anthropological data is irrelevant to metaethics
questions.explanation: (B) most directly weakens the relativist inference: if a core norm appears universally, the diversity the relativist relies on is superficial, supporting universalism. (A) would cast doubt on data reliability but not directly undermine relativism. (C) raises sociological concerns, not logical ones. (D) supports ignoring the evidence but does not show it fails to support relativism.

subquestions.stem: What inference do relativists draw from anthropological diversity?
options: That no moral code has a privileged status independent of its cultural home
That all cultures must eventually converge on the same moral norms
subquestions.explanation: Relativists use diversity to argue that no norm enjoys objective standing beyond its culture.

subquestions.stem: How would universal cross-cultural agreement on a core norm undermine this inference?
options: It would show that some norms may transcend culture, suggesting objective moral standards
It would show that cultures are more similar than anthropologists claim
subquestions.explanation: A universal norm is exactly what the universalist predicts — it undermines the claim that all norms are merely culturally local.

passage:
Few debates in literary theory have proved more durable than the question of authorial intent: does the meaning of a text reside primarily in what its author meant to communicate, or does meaning emerge through the activity of reading itself? At one extreme, the intentionalist holds that misreading is always possible — that interpretations diverging from the author's design are simply mistaken, however ingenious or culturally resonant they may be. At the other extreme, the anti-intentionalist insists that once a text is released into the world, the author's psychological states become strictly irrelevant: what the words mean is fixed not by biography but by the conventions of the language and genre in which they are embedded.

The anti-intentionalist position gained influential expression in mid-twentieth-century criticism, when theorists argued that treating the author's intention as the criterion of correct interpretation commits a category error. Evidence about what a poet hoped to achieve, they maintained, cannot settle what a poem achieves, because a poem is a verbal artifact that may realize meanings its creator did not foresee — meanings that become available only through the community of readers over time. The celebrated example of an ironic text serves as illustration: a writer may compose a scathing satire of nationalistic rhetoric while sincerely believing the rhetoric they are parodying; the satiric meaning is there in the text whether the author intended it or not.

Intentionalists counter that language is an intentional practice: utterances mean what speakers mean by them, and written language does not somehow escape this fundamental feature of linguistic communication. To read a text in complete disregard of what its author was trying to do, they argue, is to treat it as a natural object rather than a human creation — to decode it the way one might decode an accidental inkblot rather than a deliberate message. Moreover, intentionalists point out that appeals to 'the language and genre' still presuppose communicative conventions that are themselves constituted by the intentions of the linguistic community.

A mediating view distinguishes between actual authorial intention (the historical individual's specific psychological states) and what might be called the implied author: the authorial persona that a competent reader constructs from the textual evidence alone. On this account, interpretation aims neither at private biography nor at free-floating textual play but at the coherent communicative project that the text itself invites. The implied author is, in effect, the best hypothesis about what an ideally communicative author of this text would have intended — a construct that disciplines interpretation without requiring access to historical documents.

What unites all parties to this dispute is the recognition that texts are not transparent windows onto authors' minds or onto a language-independent reality. They are artifacts whose significance is never simply given but must always be construed — a fact that makes hermeneutics, the theory of interpretation, as much a practical as a philosophical discipline.

questions.stem: The passage indicates that anti-intentionalists believe the meaning of a text is determined by:
options: the conventions of language and genre in which the text is written
the biographical circumstances of the author's life
the reader's subjective emotional response to the text
the historical period in which the text was composed
questions.explanation: (A) is directly stated: anti-intentionalists hold that 'what the words mean is fixed not by biography but by the conventions of the language and genre.' (B) is the opposite — anti-intentionalists reject biography as a criterion. (C) is not stated. (D) is not mentioned as the anti-intentionalist criterion.

subquestions.stem: What do anti-intentionalists say becomes irrelevant once a text is released?
options: The author's psychological states and intentions
The linguistic conventions of the genre
subquestions.explanation: The passage explicitly states: 'the author's psychological states become strictly irrelevant.'

subquestions.stem: What do anti-intentionalists say does determine meaning?
options: The conventions of the language and genre in which the text is embedded
The consensus among professional literary critics
subquestions.explanation: The passage states meaning is 'fixed not by biography but by the conventions of the language and genre.'

questions.stem: The example of a writer who composes a satire while sincerely believing the rhetoric being parodied is used to argue that:
options: satiric meaning can exist in a text independently of the author's conscious intention
irony is impossible to achieve without the author's deliberate design
readers inevitably misread texts when authors are internally inconsistent
anti-intentionalism is incompatible with appreciating satiric literature
questions.explanation: (A) is correct: the example shows that 'the satiric meaning is there in the text whether the author intended it or not,' supporting anti-intentionalism. (B) contradicts the example. (C) is not implied. (D) is the opposite of the passage's argument.

subquestions.stem: What does the writer sincerely believe while composing the satire?
options: The nationalistic rhetoric being parodied
That the satire is a straightforward endorsement of nationalism
subquestions.explanation: The passage says the writer 'sincerely believing the rhetoric they are parodying.'

subquestions.stem: What conclusion does the passage draw about satiric meaning?
options: The satiric meaning exists in the text regardless of whether the author intended it
Satire requires that the author consciously design the ironic effect
subquestions.explanation: The passage explicitly states: 'the satiric meaning is there in the text whether the author intended it or not.'

questions.stem: Intentionalists compare reading a text without regard for authorial intent to:
options: decoding an accidental inkblot rather than a deliberate message
translating a text from one language into another
analyzing a natural landscape for symbolic meaning
judging a sculpture by the tools used to create it
questions.explanation: (A) is directly stated: ignoring intent is 'to decode it the way one might decode an accidental inkblot rather than a deliberate message.' (B), (C), and (D) are analogies not found in the passage.

subquestions.stem: What kind of object do intentionalists say a text without intent would resemble?
options: A natural object like an accidental inkblot
A social institution like a legal contract
subquestions.explanation: The passage says ignoring intent treats the text 'as a natural object... an accidental inkblot.'

subquestions.stem: Why is this comparison significant for intentionalists?
options: Because texts are human creations constituted by communicative intent, not accidents
Because inkblots have richer symbolic meaning than texts
subquestions.explanation: The contrast between a deliberate message and an accident illustrates why intent matters to interpretation.

questions.stem: The concept of the 'implied author' is presented as a middle position because it:
options: avoids both private biography and unconstrained textual play by focusing on the communicative project the text itself invites
combines information from the author's private letters with the reader's emotional reactions
treats the text as a historical document that must be decoded with period-specific tools
replaces the author's intention with the consensus interpretation of professional scholars
questions.explanation: (A) is correct: the implied author is 'the best hypothesis about what an ideally communicative author of this text would have intended' — a construct that 'disciplines interpretation without requiring access to historical documents.' (B) introduces biography and emotional reaction, both of which the implied-author view is meant to avoid. (C) conflates historical-critical method with the implied-author framework. (D) is not stated.

subquestions.stem: What two extremes does the implied-author view try to avoid?
options: Private biography and free-floating textual play
Emotional response and scholarly consensus
subquestions.explanation: The passage says interpretation aims 'neither at private biography nor at free-floating textual play.'

subquestions.stem: What does the implied author represent in this framework?
options: The best hypothesis about what an ideally communicative author of this text would have intended
The historical author's actual recorded statements about the work
subquestions.explanation: The passage defines the implied author as 'the best hypothesis about what an ideally communicative author of this text would have intended.'

questions.stem: A literary scholar argues that a nineteenth-century novel should be read in light of letters the author wrote describing her intentions for the work. With which position in the passage would this scholar most align?
options: Intentionalism, because it treats the author's psychological states as the criterion of correct interpretation
Anti-intentionalism, because it focuses on what the text achieves rather than what the author designed
The implied-author view, because it reconstructs authorial intent from textual evidence alone
No position in the passage, because the passage rejects using any biographical evidence
questions.explanation: (A) is correct: consulting the author's letters to determine her intentions is precisely the intentionalist move. (B) is the opposite; anti-intentionalism declares biography 'strictly irrelevant.' (C) is close but wrong: the implied-author view explicitly requires no 'access to historical documents' like letters. (D) is false; intentionalism does rely on biographical evidence.

subquestions.stem: What kind of evidence does the scholar in the question rely on?
options: The author's letters describing her intentions
The historical reception of the novel by contemporary readers
subquestions.explanation: The scholar uses private letters — direct biographical evidence of authorial intention.

subquestions.stem: Which position treats the author's psychological states as the key interpretive criterion?
options: Intentionalism
Anti-intentionalism
The implied-author view
subquestions.explanation: Intentionalists hold that utterances 'mean what speakers mean by them' — the author's states are decisive.

passage:
History is not the past itself but a selective reconstruction of the past, assembled from fragmentary evidence by scholars whose questions, categories, and assumptions are inevitably shaped by their own time and place. This observation, often associated with the philosophy of history that emerged in the nineteenth and twentieth centuries, does not reduce historical writing to fiction. Yet it does demand that historians acknowledge their craft as an interpretive one rather than a neutral transcription of what happened.

The most influential historians have always made choices: choices about which events to emphasize, which actors to foreground, and which causal chains to trace. A military historian who concentrates on battles and generals produces a different account of the same period than a social historian who foregrounds the lives of ordinary laborers, or an economic historian who treats fluctuations in grain prices as the deeper explanatory variable. None of these accounts is simply wrong; each illuminates a dimension of a complex past while necessarily leaving other dimensions in shadow.

Critics of this pluralism worry that it licenses an anything-goes relativism in which no historical account is better than any other. Defenders respond that recognizing the constructed character of historical narratives is compatible with rigorous standards of evidence, logical consistency, and fidelity to the archival record. An interpretation can be shown to be false if it contradicts well-authenticated documents, or shown to be weak if it ignores a large body of relevant evidence. The standards are not arbitrary, even if the questions that animate historical inquiry are themselves products of particular intellectual climates.

A further complication arises from the role of the historical archive itself. Archives are not neutral repositories of the past; they are collections shaped by decisions about what to preserve, who had the resources to produce written records, and which categories of information administrative systems deemed worth tracking. Consequently, the voices most abundantly represented in archives tend to be those of literate elites, state bureaucracies, and dominant institutions, while the experiences of the illiterate, the dispossessed, and the colonized are filtered through the records of those who observed or administered them from a position of power.

Contemporary historians have developed a range of techniques to recover marginalized perspectives: reading documents 'against the grain' of their intended purpose, triangulating between different types of sources, and drawing on oral traditions and material culture as complementary evidence. These methods have expanded the scope of historical knowledge considerably, even as they have deepened awareness that the gap between the past and the historian's account of it can never be fully closed.

questions.stem: The author's main claim about history is that it is:
options: a selective, interpretive reconstruction shaped by the historian's own context
essentially fictional because it cannot be verified against the actual past
only valid when it focuses on elites and state bureaucracies who left written records
a neutral transcription of events that speaks for itself without interpretation
questions.explanation: (A) is the explicit thesis: history is 'a selective reconstruction of the past' shaped by 'questions, categories, and assumptions' that are 'shaped by their own time and place.' (B) overstates — the passage says this does not reduce history to fiction. (C) is the opposite. (D) is what the passage explicitly rejects.

subquestions.stem: Does the passage claim that history is pure fiction?
options: No — acknowledging interpretive nature does not reduce history to fiction
Yes — it argues historical accounts are no more reliable than novels
subquestions.explanation: The passage explicitly states: 'does not reduce historical writing to fiction.'

subquestions.stem: What phrase captures the author's view of the historian's craft?
options: An interpretive craft rather than a neutral transcription
A scientific discipline that yields objective truths
subquestions.explanation: The passage says historians must acknowledge their craft 'as an interpretive one rather than a neutral transcription.'

questions.stem: According to the passage, defenders of historiographical pluralism argue that:
options: acknowledging constructed narratives is compatible with evidence-based standards of evaluation
any historical interpretation is as good as any other if sincerely believed
the archival record is always sufficient to verify a historical claim
historical truth is accessible only to scholars within dominant institutional frameworks
questions.explanation: (A) is directly stated: defenders argue that recognizing narratives are constructed is 'compatible with rigorous standards of evidence, logical consistency, and fidelity to the archival record.' (B) describes the 'anything-goes relativism' defenders are responding to. (C) is the opposite of the archive critique. (D) contradicts sympathy with recovering marginalized voices.

subquestions.stem: What standards do defenders say can still distinguish better from worse historical accounts?
options: Evidence, logical consistency, and fidelity to the archival record
Ideological alignment with progressive historical interpretations
subquestions.explanation: The passage lists: 'rigorous standards of evidence, logical consistency, and fidelity to the archival record.'

subquestions.stem: Can an interpretation be shown to be false, according to defenders?
options: Yes — if it contradicts well-authenticated documents
No — all interpretations are equally valid
subquestions.explanation: The passage says 'an interpretation can be shown to be false if it contradicts well-authenticated documents.'

questions.stem: The passage identifies a significant limitation of historical archives, which is that they:
options: over-represent literate elites and institutions while filtering out the experiences of the powerless
are too large and comprehensive for any single historian to master
deliberately destroy records that would undermine the narratives of dominant groups
are accessible only to scholars with advanced training in archival methods
questions.explanation: (A) is stated directly: archives tend to represent 'literate elites, state bureaucracies, and dominant institutions, while the experiences of the illiterate, the dispossessed, and the colonized are filtered through the records of those who... administered them from a position of power.' (B) is not discussed. (C) overstates — the passage says archives reflect who had resources, not deliberate destruction. (D) is not mentioned.

subquestions.stem: Whose voices are most abundantly represented in historical archives?
options: Literate elites, state bureaucracies, and dominant institutions
Ordinary laborers and the dispossessed
subquestions.explanation: The passage states archives tend to represent 'literate elites, state bureaucracies, and dominant institutions.'

subquestions.stem: Through whose records do the experiences of the dispossessed appear?
options: Those who observed or administered them from a position of power
Their own self-authored accounts preserved by archivists
subquestions.explanation: The passage says experiences 'are filtered through the records of those who observed or administered them from a position of power.'

questions.stem: A historian studying the lives of enslaved people in the antebellum American South relies on plantation owners' diaries and runaway-slave advertisements. This approach best exemplifies the technique the passage calls:
options: reading documents against the grain of their intended purpose
neutral transcription of what happened
triangulating between different historians' published interpretations
restricting evidence to material culture because written records are unreliable
questions.explanation: (A) is correct: plantation owners' diaries were produced for purposes other than documenting enslaved perspectives, but the historian uses them to recover those perspectives — exactly 'reading documents against the grain of their intended purpose.' (B) is what the passage rejects. (C) confuses triangulating source types with comparing secondary literature. (D) is not the strategy described — the historian uses written records.

subquestions.stem: What does 'reading against the grain' mean in the passage's framework?
options: Using documents for a purpose different from the one they were originally intended to serve
Rejecting archival documents in favor of oral traditions
subquestions.explanation: The passage defines it as 'reading documents against the grain of their intended purpose.'

subquestions.stem: Why does using plantation diaries to study enslaved lives fit this technique?
options: Because plantation diaries were written by slaveholders for their own purposes, yet the historian extracts information about enslaved people from them
Because the diaries were secretly written by enslaved people
subquestions.explanation: The diary was not intended to document enslaved lives sympathetically; using it to recover those lives is reading against the grain.

questions.stem: Different historical accounts of the same period — military, social, economic — are best described by the passage as:
options: each illuminating a different dimension of a complex past while leaving others in shadow
competing accounts only one of which can be correct
equally arbitrary, since all historical choices are subjective
valid only when they corroborate each other across all dimensions
questions.explanation: (A) is the passage's view: 'None of these accounts is simply wrong; each illuminates a dimension of a complex past while necessarily leaving other dimensions in shadow.' (B) adopts the either/or framing the passage rejects. (C) is the 'anything-goes relativism' the passage critiques. (D) is not stated.

subquestions.stem: Does the passage say any of the three historical approaches is simply wrong?
options: No — each is said to illuminate a dimension of the past
Yes — only social history is validated as most accurate
subquestions.explanation: The passage explicitly states: 'None of these accounts is simply wrong.'

subquestions.stem: What does each approach necessarily do to other dimensions of the past?
options: Leave them in shadow
Contradict or disprove them
subquestions.explanation: The passage says each illuminates one dimension 'while necessarily leaving other dimensions in shadow.'

Beauty and sublimity are often treated as synonyms in everyday speech, but the aesthetic tradition has long maintained a crucial distinction between them. Beauty, on the classical account, is associated with harmony, proportion, and pleasure: the beautiful object is one whose parts cohere in a way that the observer finds immediately gratifying. Sublimity, by contrast, involves an experience of excess — of forms or magnitudes that exceed the observer's capacity to take them in all at once. The encounter with the sublime is therefore double-edged: it produces both awe and a kind of distress, a sense of being overwhelmed that coexists with exhilaration.

Eighteenth-century theorists were among the first to treat the sublime as a distinct aesthetic category. One influential account located the cause of sublimity in attributes such as vast extent, darkness, and the suggestion of danger or power. A stormy ocean, a jagged mountain range, the interior of a vast cathedral — these provoke a response that differs qualitatively from our response to a well-proportioned garden or a graceful melody. The difference lies not in mere intensity but in the character of the response: the sublime suspends our habitual sense of comfort and security.

A subsequent philosophical tradition shifted the locus of the sublime from the external object to the observer's mental activity. On this view, what is truly sublime is not the mountain itself but the mind's discovery of its own capacity to conceive of what it cannot perceive — to think totality even when the senses are overwhelmed by the magnitude before them. The initial feeling of inadequacy thus gives way to a recognition of the mind's superiority: the faculty that can frame the idea of infinity is, in this sense, greater than any finite spectacle.

These two accounts — one locating sublimity in the object's properties, the other in the observer's cognitive response — are not necessarily incompatible. Objects with certain properties may reliably trigger the cognitive response that constitutes the full experience of the sublime. What both accounts agree on, however, is that the sublime cannot be reduced to beauty: the sublime disrupts the comfortable relationship between the observer and the world, while beauty affirms and soothes it.

Contemporary critics have extended the concept of the sublime to non-natural contexts. The technological sublime finds its occasion in artifacts of overwhelming scale or power: a vast suspension bridge, a particle accelerator, a data center housing more information than any mind could process. Whether such objects genuinely provoke the traditional experience of mingled awe and inadequacy, or whether they produce only spectacle and wonder, remains a live debate in aesthetic theory.

questions.stem: According to the passage, the key difference between beauty and sublimity is that:
options: beauty involves harmonious pleasure, while sublimity involves being overwhelmed in a way that is both distressing and exhilarating
beauty is experienced outdoors in nature, while sublimity is experienced in art galleries
beauty requires technical training to appreciate, while sublimity is universally accessible
beauty is a modern concept, while sublimity dates to ancient Greece
questions.explanation: (A) matches the passage: beauty is 'harmony, proportion, and pleasure,' while sublimity involves 'excess' and is 'double-edged: it produces both awe and a kind of distress.' (B) is not stated and contradicts the passage (a cathedral is not outdoors). (C) is not mentioned. (D) is historically inaccurate per the passage.

subquestions.stem: What does beauty primarily produce, according to the passage?
options: Immediate gratification through harmony and proportion
Awe mixed with a sense of inadequacy
subquestions.explanation: Beauty is described as 'harmony, proportion, and pleasure: the beautiful object is one whose parts cohere in a way that the observer finds immediately gratifying.'

subquestions.stem: What makes the sublime experience 'double-edged'?
options: It produces both awe and distress simultaneously
It can be found in both natural and artificial objects
subquestions.explanation: The passage says it 'produces both awe and a kind of distress, a sense of being overwhelmed that coexists with exhilaration.'

questions.stem: The philosophical tradition described in the third paragraph locates the sublime primarily in:
options: the mind's discovery of its own capacity to conceive what the senses cannot fully perceive
the external object's attributes of vastness and danger
the cultural conventions that teach observers to respond with awe
the physiological arousal produced by threatening stimuli
questions.explanation: (A) is stated directly: 'what is truly sublime is not the mountain itself but the mind's discovery of its own capacity to conceive of what it cannot perceive.' (B) describes the earlier, object-centered account. (C) introduces cultural conventions not discussed. (D) introduces physiology not mentioned.

subquestions.stem: Where does the sublime ultimately reside, according to the third-paragraph tradition?
options: In the observer's mind and its cognitive capacities
In the external object's physical properties
subquestions.explanation: The passage says 'what is truly sublime is not the mountain itself but the mind's discovery of its own capacity to conceive.'

subquestions.stem: What cognitive achievement does the mind accomplish in the sublime experience?
options: Framing the idea of infinity even when overwhelmed by a finite spectacle
Memorizing the dimensions of a vast natural landscape
subquestions.explanation: The passage says 'the faculty that can frame the idea of infinity is, in this sense, greater than any finite spectacle.'

questions.stem: The author suggests that the object-centered and observer-centered accounts of the sublime are:
options: potentially compatible, since certain object properties may reliably trigger the cognitive response
mutually exclusive theories, only one of which can be correct
identical in all important respects despite apparent surface differences
both clearly false, since the sublime is purely a cultural convention
questions.explanation: (A) is stated: 'These two accounts... are not necessarily incompatible. Objects with certain properties may reliably trigger the cognitive response.' (B) contradicts this. (C) is wrong — the passage notes a real difference in where they locate the sublime. (D) introduces cultural convention the passage does not propose.

subquestions.stem: Does the passage say the two accounts are necessarily incompatible?
options: No — it says they 'are not necessarily incompatible'
Yes — it treats them as rival theories
subquestions.explanation: The passage explicitly states 'These two accounts are not necessarily incompatible.'

subquestions.stem: How might the two accounts be reconciled?
options: Objects with sublime properties may reliably trigger the cognitive response that constitutes the experience
Observers must be trained to find objects sublime regardless of their properties
subquestions.explanation: The passage says 'Objects with certain properties may reliably trigger the cognitive response that constitutes the full experience of the sublime.'

questions.stem: The passage implies the sublime cannot be reduced to beauty primarily because:
options: the sublime disrupts the comfortable relationship between observer and world, while beauty affirms it
the sublime is always triggered by natural objects, while beauty arises from art
the sublime is more intense than beauty and therefore distinct only in degree
beauty is appreciated collectively, while the sublime is an irreducibly private experience
questions.explanation: (A) is stated explicitly: 'the sublime cannot be reduced to beauty: the sublime disrupts the comfortable relationship between the observer and the world, while beauty affirms and soothes it.' (B) contradicts the passage (cathedrals are man-made; data centers are technological). (C) is incorrect — the difference is in 'character,' not merely intensity. (D) is not mentioned.

subquestions.stem: What does the sublime do to the observer's relationship with the world?
options: Disrupts it
Affirms and soothes it
subquestions.explanation: The passage says 'the sublime disrupts the comfortable relationship between the observer and the world.'

subquestions.stem: What does beauty do to that same relationship?
options: Affirms and soothes it
Disrupts and overwhelms it
subquestions.explanation: The passage contrasts: beauty 'affirms and soothes' the comfortable relationship.

questions.stem: A reader visits a massive data center and feels both awed by its scale and vaguely threatened by the incomprehensibility of the information it contains. According to the passage's discussion of the technological sublime, this experience:
options: fits the traditional description of the sublime as mingled awe and inadequacy, illustrating why critics extend the concept to technological artifacts
proves that the technological sublime is identical to the natural sublime
demonstrates that beauty and sublimity are the same in technological contexts
cannot be a genuine aesthetic experience because it involves artificial, not natural, objects
questions.explanation: (A) is correct: the passage describes the traditional sublime as 'mingled awe and inadequacy' and notes contemporary critics debate whether technological objects 'genuinely provoke the traditional experience of mingled awe and inadequacy.' The reader's response fits. (B) is not stated. (C) contradicts the core distinction. (D) is exactly the view the passage disputes by raising the technological sublime as a live topic.

subquestions.stem: How does the passage describe the traditional experience of the sublime?
options: As mingled awe and inadequacy
As pure pleasure with no element of distress
subquestions.explanation: The passage uses 'mingled awe and inadequacy' and earlier 'both awe and a kind of distress.'

subquestions.stem: What open question does the passage raise about technological objects and the sublime?
options: Whether they genuinely provoke the traditional experience or merely produce spectacle and wonder
Whether they are aesthetically inferior to natural landscapes
subquestions.explanation: The passage says 'Whether such objects genuinely provoke the traditional experience of mingled awe and inadequacy, or whether they produce only spectacle and wonder, remains a live debate.'

passage:
Social capital — broadly understood as the networks of relationships, norms of reciprocity, and trust that enable collective action — has become one of the most debated concepts in contemporary sociology. Proponents argue that it explains why some communities flourish economically and politically while others, equally well endowed in financial and physical resources, remain mired in dysfunction. Where citizens trust one another and are embedded in overlapping civic associations, cooperative ventures are easier to organize, contracts are more reliably honored, and public goods are more effectively maintained.

The concept distinguishes between two broad types. Bonding social capital refers to the dense ties within homogeneous groups — extended families, ethnic neighborhoods, religious congregations — that provide strong mutual support and a sense of belonging. Bridging social capital refers to the weaker but more diverse ties that link members of different social groups, enabling information and resources to flow across community boundaries. Theorists have argued that bridging capital is especially important for economic mobility and democratic stability, because it prevents the insularity and exclusivity that can calcify into tribal antagonism.

Critics have raised several concerns. First, they argue that the concept is so capacious as to be analytically vacuous: if social capital includes trust, networks, and norms all at once, it risks becoming a label that explains everything and therefore explains nothing. Second, the emphasis on voluntary civic associations may obscure the role of institutional design and state capacity in producing the outcomes attributed to social capital. A society with strong courts, reliable public services, and enforceable property rights may achieve cooperation through formal mechanisms, regardless of the density of its associational life.

A third critique focuses on the dark side of social capital. The same dense ties that produce cohesion within a group can generate exclusion and hostility toward outsiders. Historically, tightly bonded criminal networks, nativist movements, and exclusionary professional guilds have all exhibited strong social capital while causing harm to those outside the charmed circle. If social capital is a neutral resource that amplifies whatever goals a group pursues, then celebrating it uncritically is a mistake. A community of mutual trust can be a community of mutual hostility toward all who fall outside its boundaries, and the literature on ethnic cleansing, organized crime, and racially restrictive covenants demonstrates this possibility in grim historical detail.

These debates have produced a more nuanced research agenda. Scholars now investigate not just the presence or absence of social capital but its distribution across social groups, its articulation with formal institutions, and the conditions under which it promotes rather than undermines democratic participation and social equity.

questions.stem: According to the passage, bridging social capital is considered especially important for democratic stability because it:
options: prevents insularity that can harden into tribal antagonism by linking members of different groups
provides the dense mutual support needed for communities to survive economic shocks
ensures that all members of a community share the same norms and values
creates formal legal institutions that enforce contracts and property rights
questions.explanation: (A) is directly stated: bridging capital 'prevents the insularity and exclusivity that can calcify into tribal antagonism.' (B) describes bonding capital, not bridging. (C) is the opposite of bridging capital — shared norms within a group is bonding. (D) describes formal institutions, which the third paragraph distinguishes from social capital.

subquestions.stem: What does bridging social capital link?
options: Members of different social groups
Members of the same homogeneous group
subquestions.explanation: Bridging capital 'link[s] members of different social groups, enabling information and resources to flow across community boundaries.'

subquestions.stem: What risk does bridging capital help prevent?
options: Insularity and exclusivity that can calcify into tribal antagonism
Over-reliance on formal legal institutions
subquestions.explanation: The passage says bridging capital 'prevents the insularity and exclusivity that can calcify into tribal antagonism.'

questions.stem: The critique that social capital is 'analytically vacuous' is based on the claim that:
options: a concept encompassing trust, networks, and norms simultaneously explains too much and therefore too little
social capital cannot be measured in quantitative sociological studies
trust and networks have been shown empirically to have no effect on community outcomes
the concept was developed by researchers with ideological rather than scientific motivations
questions.explanation: (A) is the exact critique: 'if social capital includes trust, networks, and norms all at once, it risks becoming a label that explains everything and therefore explains nothing.' (B) is a methodological concern not raised. (C) contradicts the passage. (D) introduces ideological motives not mentioned.

subquestions.stem: What specific problem does the 'analytically vacuous' critique identify?
options: The concept is too broad, encompassing too many different things at once
The concept was borrowed from economics and does not apply to sociology
subquestions.explanation: The critique is that including 'trust, networks, and norms all at once' makes the concept a label that 'explains everything and therefore explains nothing.'

subquestions.stem: What is the logical structure of the 'explains everything, explains nothing' criticism?
options: A concept with no exclusions cannot be tested or falsified because any outcome confirms it
A concept that is too specific cannot explain diverse social phenomena
subquestions.explanation: If a label can explain every outcome, it makes no predictions that could be proven wrong — rendering it untestable.

questions.stem: The 'dark side of social capital' critique most directly challenges which assumption underlying the concept's positive reception?
options: That social capital reliably promotes beneficial outcomes for society as a whole
That bridging capital is more valuable than bonding capital
That formal institutions are inferior to voluntary associations in generating cooperation
That social capital varies significantly across communities
questions.explanation: (A) is correct: the dark-side critique argues that dense ties can produce exclusion and harm outsiders, undermining the assumption that social capital is universally beneficial. (B) is not directly challenged by this critique. (C) is the second critique, not the dark-side critique. (D) is a sociological observation the passage does not dispute.

subquestions.stem: What examples does the passage give of groups with high social capital that caused harm?
options: Criminal networks, nativist movements, and exclusionary professional guilds
Religious congregations and extended families
subquestions.explanation: The passage names 'tightly bonded criminal networks, nativist movements, and exclusionary professional guilds.'

subquestions.stem: What does the dark-side critique imply about uncritical celebration of social capital?
options: It is a mistake, because social capital amplifies whatever goals a group pursues
It is justified, because the benefits always outweigh the harms
subquestions.explanation: The passage states: 'If social capital is a neutral resource that amplifies whatever goals a group pursues, then celebrating it uncritically is a mistake.'

questions.stem: A city with low voluntary associational activity but strong courts, reliable police, and efficient public services achieves low corruption and high civic cooperation. This scenario most directly supports which critique of social capital theory?
options: That formal institutional design may produce cooperative outcomes independently of social capital
That bridging capital cannot exist without strong bonding capital as a foundation
That social capital is analytically vacuous because it defines terms too broadly
That the dark side of social capital makes it net-negative for democratic societies
questions.explanation: (A) is correct: the second critique argues that 'a society with strong courts, reliable public services, and enforceable property rights may achieve cooperation through formal mechanisms, regardless of the density of its associational life.' (B) is not a claim in the passage. (C) concerns conceptual breadth, not empirical outcomes. (D) concerns dark-side harms, not institutional mechanisms.

subquestions.stem: What does the second critique argue can substitute for social capital?
options: Strong formal institutions such as courts and reliable public services
Increased investment in voluntary civic associations
subquestions.explanation: The critique says 'a society with strong courts, reliable public services... may achieve cooperation through formal mechanisms.'

subquestions.stem: Does the scenario support or challenge the positive view of social capital?
options: Challenges it, by showing that good outcomes can occur without high social capital
Supports it, by showing that formal institutions build social capital
subquestions.explanation: The city achieves cooperation without dense voluntary associations, suggesting social capital is not necessary.

questions.stem: The passage implies that the more nuanced research agenda described in the final paragraph arose primarily because:
options: the critiques identified in paragraphs three and four revealed significant limitations in earlier approaches
scholars discovered that social capital is irrelevant to economic development
government agencies began funding research into civic associations for policy purposes
the distinction between bonding and bridging capital was proven empirically false
questions.explanation: (A) is implied: 'These debates have produced a more nuanced research agenda' — 'these debates' refers to the critiques. (B) contradicts the passage. (C) is not mentioned. (D) contradicts the passage, which continues to use the bonding/bridging distinction.

subquestions.stem: What does 'these debates' refer to in the final paragraph?
options: The critiques about analytical vacuity, formal institutions, and the dark side of social capital
The debates between sociologists and economists about whether trust is measurable
subquestions.explanation: The passage transitions with 'These debates have produced a more nuanced research agenda,' referring back to paragraphs three and four.

subquestions.stem: What new questions does the nuanced agenda investigate?
options: Distribution of social capital, relation to formal institutions, and conditions under which it promotes democracy
Historical origins of social capital in pre-modern European societies
subquestions.explanation: The passage lists: 'distribution across social groups, articulation with formal institutions, and conditions under which it promotes... democratic participation.'

Democratic legitimacy, on one influential account, does not derive from the mere aggregation of pre-formed preferences through voting. Rather, it emerges from the quality of the public reasoning that precedes and informs collective decisions. This deliberative ideal holds that citizens who engage in open, reciprocal, and reasoned discourse — genuinely considering one another's arguments rather than simply advocating for prior commitments — are more likely to reach outcomes that are justifiable to all affected parties, regardless of who ultimately wins the vote.

The deliberative model places considerable demands on participants. It requires a willingness to offer reasons that others can evaluate on their merits, not just expressions of passion or assertions of power. It requires a disposition to revise one's views in response to compelling counter-arguments. And it requires conditions of rough equality: if some participants lack the resources, time, or rhetorical confidence to engage effectively, the deliberation is skewed in ways that may reproduce rather than remedy existing inequalities of power.

Critics have challenged the deliberative ideal on several grounds. The most fundamental challenge concerns the status of passionate advocacy. Much of what moves democratic politics — the eloquence of a civil-rights speech, the moral urgency communicated by a protest march, the experiential testimony of those who have suffered injustice — does not take the form of a reasoned argument that invites calm evaluation. To require that all democratic communication conform to deliberative norms risks excluding the voices of the marginalized, whose experiences may be most authentically expressed through modes other than academic debate.

A second critique concerns the domain of the reasonable. Deliberative theory assumes that despite deep disagreements, a set of publicly acceptable reasons can be identified that citizens holding different moral and religious convictions can endorse. Critics argue this assumption is empirically dubious in societies characterized by deep moral pluralism: when citizens disagree not just about policies but about the very framework within which policies should be evaluated, appeals to 'public reason' may function to privilege particular substantive views disguised as neutral procedural ones.

Defenders of deliberative democracy acknowledge these tensions but argue they point to the need for a more expansive and less rationalist conception of deliberation — one that includes narrative, testimony, and emotional appeal alongside discursive argument — rather than an abandonment of the deliberative ideal itself. If the goal is collective decision-making that citizens can recognize as fair, some approximation of reasoned exchange remains indispensable, even if the form that exchange takes must be more capacious than classical deliberative theory imagined.

questions.stem: According to the deliberative ideal described in the passage, democratic legitimacy comes from:
options: the quality of public reasoning that precedes and informs collective decisions
the numerical majority achieved through fair and transparent vote-counting
the equal distribution of political power among all social groups
the charisma and eloquence of political leaders who can unify citizens
questions.explanation: (A) is stated directly: legitimacy 'emerges from the quality of the public reasoning that precedes and informs collective decisions.' (B) describes aggregative democracy, which the passage contrasts with deliberative democracy. (C) is not the deliberative model's account of legitimacy. (D) is not part of the deliberative ideal.

subquestions.stem: What does the passage contrast with the deliberative model's account of legitimacy?
options: The mere aggregation of pre-formed preferences through voting
The enforcement of constitutional rights by courts
subquestions.explanation: The passage says legitimacy 'does not derive from the mere aggregation of pre-formed preferences through voting.'

subquestions.stem: What quality of public reasoning matters?
options: Open, reciprocal, and genuinely reasoned discourse that considers others' arguments
The persuasiveness of professional rhetoricians hired by political parties
subquestions.explanation: The passage specifies: 'open, reciprocal, and reasoned discourse — genuinely considering one another's arguments.'

questions.stem: Unequal access to deliberative participation is a problem according to the passage because it:
options: may reproduce rather than remedy existing power inequalities
violates the procedural requirement that all votes count equally
ensures that majorities always impose their views on minorities
reduces the number of citizens who engage in civic associations
questions.explanation: (A) is stated: if participation is unequal, 'the deliberation is skewed in ways that may reproduce rather than remedy existing inequalities of power.' (B) conflates deliberation with voting. (C) is not the claim made. (D) relates to social capital themes not discussed here.

subquestions.stem: What conditions does the deliberative model require for legitimate deliberation?
options: Rough equality so that all participants can engage effectively
Professional moderation by trained facilitators
subquestions.explanation: The passage says deliberation 'requires conditions of rough equality: if some participants lack the resources... the deliberation is skewed.'

subquestions.stem: What happens when some participants lack resources, time, or rhetorical confidence?
options: Deliberation may reproduce rather than remedy existing power inequalities
Citizens tend to vote with economic interests rather than considered judgments
subquestions.explanation: The passage warns the deliberation becomes 'skewed in ways that may reproduce rather than remedy existing inequalities of power.'

questions.stem: Critics who argue that requiring 'public reason' privileges certain substantive views are primarily concerned that:
options: neutral procedural norms may actually disguise particular moral and religious commitments
public deliberation inevitably becomes dominated by professional politicians
citizens are unwilling to revise their views after hearing counter-arguments
majority decisions always fail to respect the rights of religious minorities
questions.explanation: (A) is the critique: 'appeals to public reason may function to privilege particular substantive views disguised as neutral procedural ones.' (B) introduces politicians not mentioned. (C) concerns willingness to revise, discussed as a demand in paragraph two. (D) introduces majority/minority rights, not the public-reason critique.

subquestions.stem: What does the second critique say about the assumption that publicly acceptable reasons exist?
options: The assumption is empirically dubious in deeply pluralistic societies
Such reasons always exist but are difficult to articulate simply
subquestions.explanation: The passage says the assumption is 'empirically dubious in societies characterized by deep moral pluralism.'

subquestions.stem: What does it mean for 'public reason' to privilege a substantive view 'disguised as neutral'?
options: One moral or religious framework gets treated as the universally acceptable standard when it actually reflects a particular worldview
Politicians use technical language to confuse citizens about policy details
subquestions.explanation: The critique is that 'public reason' claims neutrality but actually encodes one framework's assumptions as the default.

questions.stem: A grassroots campaign uses personal storytelling by formerly incarcerated people to shift public opinion on criminal justice reform, deliberately avoiding statistical arguments. A defender of the deliberative ideal would most likely say this campaign:
options: is consistent with an expanded deliberative model that includes narrative and testimony alongside discursive argument
undermines democratic legitimacy because it relies on emotion rather than reasoned argument
is the most powerful form of deliberation because stories are more persuasive than statistics
is only legitimate if the storytellers also provide policy proposals based on empirical evidence
questions.explanation: (A) is correct: defenders call for 'a more expansive and less rationalist conception of deliberation — one that includes narrative, testimony, and emotional appeal alongside discursive argument.' Personal storytelling exemplifies this. (B) represents the narrow rationalist view critics are challenging. (C) is not the passage's claim. (D) introduces a requirement the passage's defenders do not make.

subquestions.stem: What do defenders of deliberative democracy argue in the final paragraph?
options: Deliberation should be expanded to include narrative, testimony, and emotional appeal, not just discursive argument
Deliberation should be restricted to formal academic debate to ensure rigor
subquestions.explanation: Defenders call for 'a more expansive and less rationalist conception of deliberation — one that includes narrative, testimony, and emotional appeal.'

subquestions.stem: Does the final paragraph say the deliberative ideal should be abandoned?
options: No — it calls for a revised, more expansive conception, not abandonment
Yes — the critiques are said to be decisive against deliberative theory
subquestions.explanation: The defenders argue for revision 'rather than an abandonment of the deliberative ideal itself.'

questions.stem: The passage's description of deliberative democracy's demands implies that a deliberative process would be corrupted when participants:
options: assert power or passion instead of offering reasons others can evaluate on their merits
vote according to their sincere preferences after listening to all arguments
invoke constitutional principles as reasons for their positions
change their minds after hearing arguments from the opposing side
questions.explanation: (A) is the inverse of the demand stated: deliberation requires offering 'reasons that others can evaluate on their merits, not just expressions of passion or assertions of power.' When participants substitute passion for reasons, deliberation is corrupted. (B) is expected endpoint behavior. (C) is a form of reason, not corruption. (D) is the desired behavior per the passage.

subquestions.stem: What kind of communication does the deliberative model require?
options: Reasons that others can evaluate on their merits
Passionate rhetoric that moves fellow citizens to action
subquestions.explanation: The model requires 'a willingness to offer reasons that others can evaluate on their merits, not just expressions of passion or assertions of power.'

subquestions.stem: What disposition must deliberators have toward opposing arguments?
options: A willingness to revise their views in response to compelling counter-arguments
A commitment to maintaining their initial positions regardless of counterevidence
subquestions.explanation: The passage says deliberation 'requires a disposition to revise one's views in response to compelling counter-arguments.'

passage:
Ritual occupies a paradoxical place in modern social thought. On one hand, it has been systematically studied as a fundamental mechanism of social solidarity: shared symbolic performances bind participants together, express and reinforce collective values, and mark the transitions — birth, initiation, marriage, death — that punctuate the human life course.
